vocabulary.meaning: to state that something is not true
vocabulary.example: He denied taking the money from the cash register.

vocabulary.meaning: to say that you will not do or accept something
vocabulary.example: She refused to answer any more questions.

vocabulary.meaning: to have the same opinion as someone else
vocabulary.example: I agree with you that the plan is too risky.

vocabulary.meaning: to agree to receive or take something
vocabulary.example: He accepted the job offer.

vocabulary.meaning: the title of a newspaper article printed in large letters
vocabulary.example: The headlines on the front page were about the election.

vocabulary.meaning: a title at the head of a page or section of a book
vocabulary.example: The teacher asked us to write the heading in our notebooks.

vocabulary.meaning: a special article or programme about a particular subject
vocabulary.example: The magazine has a special feature on modern technology.

vocabulary.meaning: a piece of writing in a newspaper or magazine
vocabulary.example: I read an interesting article about space travel.

vocabulary.meaning: a television or radio programme in which celebrities are interviewed
vocabulary.example: She was invited to appear on a popular late-night talk show.

vocabulary.meaning: a television show in which contestants answer questions
vocabulary.example: He won a million dollars on a TV quiz show.

vocabulary.meaning: a television programme in which people play games to win prizes
vocabulary.example: My family loves watching game shows on Sunday evenings.

vocabulary.meaning: a person who introduces programmes or reads news on TV or radio
vocabulary.example: The television announcer introduced the next show.

vocabulary.meaning: a person who describes a sports event or situation on TV or radio
vocabulary.example: The football commentator was very excited during the match.

vocabulary.meaning: a popular newspaper with small pages and short, simple articles
vocabulary.example: Tabloids usually focus on gossip about celebrities.

vocabulary.meaning: a serious newspaper printed on large sheets of paper
vocabulary.example: Broadsheets provide more detailed international news than tabloids.

vocabulary.meaning: a person who writes news reports for newspapers, TV, or radio
vocabulary.example: The journalist interviewed the prime minister.

vocabulary.meaning: a journalist who writes a regular series of articles for a newspaper
vocabulary.example: She is a well-known columnist for a national newspaper.

vocabulary.meaning: newspapers, magazines, and the journalists who work for them
vocabulary.example: The press was not allowed to attend the private meeting.

vocabulary.meaning: television, radio, newspapers, and the internet as a group
vocabulary.example: The news media plays an important role in society.

vocabulary.meaning: a broadcast show on television or radio
vocabulary.example: What is your favorite television programme?

vocabulary.meaning: a set of instructions that tells a computer what to do
vocabulary.example: He wrote a computer program to organize his files.

vocabulary.meaning: a television station and the programmes it broadcasts
vocabulary.example: Please change the channel; I want to watch the news.

vocabulary.meaning: a television or radio programme or transmission
vocabulary.example: The broadcast of the concert was watched by millions.

vocabulary.meaning: a short official statement of news or information
vocabulary.example: The news bulletin gave the latest details on the storm.

vocabulary.meaning: a short news report that interrupts a television or radio programme
vocabulary.example: We interrupt this broadcast for an important newsflash.

vocabulary.meaning: the act of participating or being connected with something
vocabulary.example: The police are investigating his involvement in the robbery.

vocabulary.meaning: an event that causes public outrage or shock
vocabulary.example: The politician was forced to resign after the financial scandal.

vocabulary.meaning: a large amount or number of something
vocabulary.example: I have loads of things to do before we leave.

vocabulary.meaning: conversation or reports about other people's private lives
vocabulary.example: She loves reading celebrity gossip in magazines.

vocabulary.meaning: the group of people who are elected to make laws for a country
vocabulary.example: The new laws were debated in Parliament yesterday.

vocabulary.meaning: modern, or relating to the present time
vocabulary.example: The museum exhibits contemporary art by local artists.

vocabulary.meaning: a person who writes novels
vocabulary.example: Charles Dickens was a famous English novelist.

vocabulary.meaning: a formal discussion on a particular topic
vocabulary.example: The school held a debate on environmental protection.

vocabulary.meaning: a preference or prejudice for or against something
vocabulary.example: The report was criticized for political bias.

vocabulary.meaning: to be a candidate in an election
vocabulary.example: He decided to stand for election in his local district.

vocabulary.meaning: sometimes, but not regularly or often
vocabulary.example: We occasionally go out for dinner on weekends.

vocabulary.meaning: the set of basic laws and principles that a country is governed by
vocabulary.example: The country is drafting a new democratic constitution.

vocabulary.meaning: an economic and political union of European countries
vocabulary.example: The UK voted to leave the European Union.

vocabulary.meaning: a person who is an expert on the relationship between food and health
vocabulary.example: The nutritionist advised him to eat more vegetables.

vocabulary.meaning: a set of instructions for preparing a particular dish
vocabulary.example: My grandmother gave me her secret recipe for chocolate cake.

vocabulary.meaning: the direct communication of thoughts from one mind to another
vocabulary.example: She claims to communicate with her twin sister through telepathy.

vocabulary.meaning: the person who has responsibility for the editorial policy of a publication
vocabulary.example: He was promoted to editorial director of the publishing house.

vocabulary.meaning: a person who takes part in a contest or competition
vocabulary.example: Each contestant has to answer ten questions in the first round.

vocabulary.meaning: extremely, or in a way that is difficult to believe
vocabulary.example: The math exam was incredibly difficult.

vocabulary.meaning: extremely large in size or amount
vocabulary.example: They spent an enormous amount of money on the party.

vocabulary.meaning: written works, especially those considered of superior or lasting artistic merit
vocabulary.example: He is studying English literature at university.

vocabulary.meaning: a person who writes poems
vocabulary.example: William Shakespeare was a famous poet and playwright.

vocabulary.meaning: a photographer who takes pictures for newspapers or magazines
vocabulary.example: The press photographer was trying to take a picture of the actor.

vocabulary.meaning: to arrive or appear, especially unexpectedly
vocabulary.example: She turned up at the party without an invitation.

vocabulary.meaning: a famous person, especially in entertainment or sport
vocabulary.example: The hotel was packed with movie stars and celebrities.

phrasalVerbs.meaning: start discussing a subject, or raise a child
phrasalVerbs.example: EX: Don't bring up the budget problem during the meeting.

phrasalVerbs.meaning: start playing or broadcasting (for a TV programme)
phrasalVerbs.example: EX: The film comes on in ten minutes.

phrasalVerbs.meaning: be published (for a book, magazine, etc.)
phrasalVerbs.example: EX: When does the next edition of the magazine come out?

phrasalVerbs.meaning: write information in the spaces on a form
phrasalVerbs.example: EX: Please fill in this application form and return it to us.

phrasalVerbs.meaning: turn the pages of a book or magazine quickly without reading in detail
phrasalVerbs.example: EX: I flicked through the catalog while waiting at the office.

phrasalVerbs.meaning: deal with or explain something in detail
phrasalVerbs.example: EX: The article doesn't go into detail about the incident.

phrasalVerbs.meaning: distribute something to a group of people
phrasalVerbs.example: EX: The teacher handed out the exam papers to the students.

phrasalVerbs.meaning: find information in a reference book or online
phrasalVerbs.example: EX: If you don't know the word, look it up in the dictionary.

phrasalVerbs.meaning: pretend that something is true, or see/hear with difficulty
phrasalVerbs.example: EX: He made out that he was a doctor, but he wasn't.

phrasalVerbs.meaning: invent an explanation, story, or excuse
phrasalVerbs.example: EX: I don't believe him; he's just making up excuses.

phrasalVerbs.meaning: suggest an idea, plan, or proposal for consideration
phrasalVerbs.example: EX: The committee put forward several proposals for the new park.

phrasalVerbs.meaning: realize the truth about someone who is trying to deceive you
phrasalVerbs.example: EX: I immediately saw through his lies.

phrasalVerbs.meaning: be very easy to see or notice
phrasalVerbs.example: EX: She stands out in the crowd because of her bright yellow jacket.

phrasalVerbs.meaning: turn a page or card so that the other side is facing up
phrasalVerbs.example: EX: Turn over the sheet and write your name at the top.

phrasalVerbs.meaning: learn how to do or use something
phrasalVerbs.example: EX: You will soon get the hang of using this software.

collocations.meaning: having power to direct or manage something
assume power to direct or manage something
being managed successfully without problems
being directed or managed by someone
unable to be managed or directed
explain what something or someone is like
have an effect or influence on something or someone
recognize how things are not the same
things are identical or very similar
fail to appreciate the value of something because you are familiar with it
affect or change the way someone behaves or thinks
using the global computer network
look at different websites on the web
being reported or discussed in newspapers and on TV
broadcast on a news programme
receive information about recent events
happen, especially after being planned
instead of someone or something else
located in a specific position
being discussed, or being doubtful
a symbol showing that something is a question or is uncertain
possess an opinion about something
be someone's opinion that
in my opinion
because of or considering something
look at the scenery from a particular place
scenery or opinion from a particular point
the scene that you can see from a place
look at someone or something for a period of time
be careful and look for someone or something
guard or monitor a situation to prevent danger

wordPatterns.meaning: as stated by someone
wordPatterns.example: EX: According to the news, the strike has ended.

wordPatterns.meaning: declare something officially to someone
wordPatterns.example: EX: They announced their engagement to their friends.

wordPatterns.meaning: declare officially that a situation is true
wordPatterns.example: EX: The company announced that sales had increased by 10%.

wordPatterns.meaning: trust the existence or truth of something
wordPatterns.example:
EX: Do you believe in ghosts?
EX: I believe that honesty is the best policy.

wordPatterns.meaning: think that someone or something is a certain way
wordPatterns.example: EX: The manuscript is believed to be genuine.

wordPatterns.meaning: express an opinion about something
wordPatterns.example: EX: She commented on the new policy.

wordPatterns.verb: make a comment to sb about sth
wordPatterns.meaning: say something about a topic to someone
wordPatterns.example: EX: He made a comment to his boss about the working hours.

wordPatterns.meaning: mix up one thing or person with another
wordPatterns.example: EX: People often confuse me with my twin sister.

wordPatterns.meaning: feel uncertain or mixed up about something
wordPatterns.example: EX: I am confused about what to do next.

wordPatterns.meaning: exchange letters or emails with someone, or match something
wordPatterns.example: EX: She has corresponded with her pen pal for five years.

wordPatterns.meaning: say what something or someone is like
wordPatterns.example: EX: She described the thief as a tall man with black hair.

wordPatterns.meaning: receive sounds through your ears
wordPatterns.example: EX: Did you hear that strange noise?

wordPatterns.meaning: be told or find out news about something
wordPatterns.example: EX: Have you heard about the new library?

wordPatterns.meaning: receive a letter, call, or message from someone
wordPatterns.example: EX: I haven't heard from him in months.

wordPatterns.meaning: tell someone officially that a situation is true
wordPatterns.example: EX: We regret to inform you that your application was rejected.

wordPatterns.meaning: give someone details about something
wordPatterns.example: EX: Please inform us of any changes to your address.

wordPatterns.meaning: probable to perform an action
wordPatterns.example: EX: It is likely to rain this afternoon.

wordPatterns.meaning: probable that a situation will happen
wordPatterns.example: EX: It is likely that the price of petrol will rise.

wordPatterns.meaning: make someone do something by giving them good reasons
wordPatterns.example: EX: She persuaded him to join the gym.

wordPatterns.meaning: convince someone that a statement is true
wordPatterns.example: EX: I managed to persuade her that I was telling the truth.

wordPatterns.meaning: make someone believe that something is true
wordPatterns.example: EX: He tried to persuade the jury of his innocence.

wordPatterns.meaning: purpose or reason for performing an action
wordPatterns.example: EX: There is no point in waiting here any longer.

wordPatterns.meaning: arrange for something to be delivered directly to someone
wordPatterns.example: EX: My parents sent me a parcel for my birthday.

wordPatterns.meaning: arrange for something to be delivered to someone
wordPatterns.example: EX: She sent a letter to her friend.

wordPatterns.meaning: unexpectedly, causing astonishment
wordPatterns.example: EX: The sudden rain caught us by surprise.

wordPatterns.meaning: feeling astonishment because of something unexpected
wordPatterns.example: EX: I was surprised by her reaction.

wordPatterns.meaning: give information to someone by speaking or writing
wordPatterns.example: EX: He told me a secret.

wordPatterns.meaning: give someone a specific piece of information
wordPatterns.example: EX: She told me that she was leaving.

wordPatterns.meaning: talk to someone about a topic or action
wordPatterns.example: EX: He told us about his holiday in Spain.

wordPatterns.meaning: order someone to do or not do something
wordPatterns.example: EX: The doctor told me to drink more water.

wordFormation.meaning: announce: state something officially or publicly / announcement: a formal public statement / announcer: person who introduces programmes on TV or radio / unannounced: happening without warning or announcement
wordFormation.example:
EX: We announced the winner.
EX: She read the announcement.
EX: The announcer introduced the show.
EX: He made an unannounced visit.

wordFormation.meaning: believe: think that something is true / belief: feeling that something is true / disbelief: feeling of not being able to believe something / believable: easy to believe / unbelievable: hard to believe
wordFormation.example:
EX: I believe in honesty.
EX: It is my belief that he is right.
EX: She listened in disbelief.
EX: The story was believable.
EX: His progress was unbelievable.

wordFormation.meaning: communicate: share information with other people / communication: the act of sharing information / communicator: person who shares information / communicative: willing to talk and give info / uncommunicative: not willing to talk and give info
wordFormation.example:
EX: They communicate by email.
EX: Good communication is vital.
EX: He is a good communicator.
EX: She is very communicative.
EX: He was silent and uncommunicative.

wordFormation.meaning: convince: persuade someone / convinced: completely sure / convincing: able to make you believe / unconvincing: failing to make you believe
wordFormation.example:
EX: I convinced him to join us.
EX: I am convinced she is innocent.
EX: He gave a convincing argument.
EX: The evidence was unconvincing.

wordFormation.meaning: discuss: talk about something to share ideas / discussion: the process of talking about something
wordFormation.example:
EX: We discussed the new plan.
EX: The discussion lasted for two hours.

wordFormation.meaning: edit: prepare text or film by correcting / editor: person who corrects text or film / editorial: newspaper article expressing editor's opinion / editorial (adj): relating to editing / edited: corrected for publication
wordFormation.example:
EX: She edited the manuscript.
EX: He works as a book editor.
EX: The newspaper published an editorial.
EX: She made some editorial changes.
EX: Here is the edited version of the text.

wordFormation.meaning: humour: the quality of being funny / humorous: funny and making you laugh / humorless: not showing any sense of humour
wordFormation.example:
EX: I appreciate his sense of humour.
EX: He told a humorous story.
EX: She gave a humorless smile.

wordFormation.meaning: inform: give someone information / information: facts or details / informed: having knowledge / uninformed: not having knowledge / informative: providing useful info / uninformative: not providing useful info
wordFormation.example:
EX: We informed the police of the threat.
EX: I need more information.
EX: She is well informed about the subject.
EX: They were uninformed about the risks.
EX: The brochure was very informative.
EX: The report was short and uninformative.

wordFormation.meaning: journal: newspaper or magazine on a subject / journalist: person who writes news reports / journalism: the work of writing news / journalistic: relating to journalism
wordFormation.example:
EX: He writes articles for a medical journal.
EX: She works as a freelance journalist.
EX: He is studying journalism.
EX: She has a journalistic style of writing.

wordFormation.meaning: politics: activities relating to power in a country / politician: person who works in politics / political: relating to politics
wordFormation.example:
EX: He is interested in local politics.
EX: The politician gave a speech.
EX: They had a political discussion.

wordFormation.meaning: power: control or influence over people / empower: give someone authority / powerful: having a lot of power / powerless: having no power
wordFormation.example:
EX: The president has a lot of power.
EX: The law empowers the police to search suspects.
EX: He is a powerful businessman.
EX: Without help, we are powerless.

wordFormation.meaning: ridicule: words that make someone look silly / ridiculousness: the quality of being silly / ridiculous: very silly or unreasonable
wordFormation.example:
EX: His ideas were met with ridicule.
EX: We laughed at the ridiculousness of the situation.
EX: It was a ridiculous suggestion.

wordFormation.meaning: second: coming next after first, or unit of time / secondly: introducing the second point / secondary: less important than something else
wordFormation.example:
EX: It takes only a second to open it.
EX: Secondly, we need to consider the cost.
EX: Education is of secondary importance to health.

wordFormation.meaning: write: produce words on a surface / written: expressed in writing / unwritten: not written down but accepted
wordFormation.example:
EX: She wrote a letter to her parents.
EX: We have a written agreement.
EX: There is an unwritten rule about dress code.
